task: I’m thinking about moving into post-production work in Los Angeles and want a realistic sense of the entry path, especially for media/entertainment IT-engineer-type roles. Please start on Google and look up what employers in media and entertainment usually expect for IT engineers, then pull together at least three recurring requirements you keep seeing and at least two concrete training or certification routes, because I want to know whether this is something I could realistically train into. Once you have that baseline, go to the Motion Picture Editors Guild site and find the actual West Coast or Los Angeles path for joining IATSE Local 700, including the steps, eligibility, and anything about applications, rosters, fees, or required experience, so I can compare the union route with the broader training path. If there are separate pages that matter, open the key Local 700 pages in their own tabs and leave the most useful one open so I can look at the exact wording myself. After that, go back to Google and search for current Los Angeles or broader West Coast jobs that actually match the skills and requirements you found earlier, and open at least two relevant live job postings in separate tabs so I can visually compare them; for each one, note the title, company, location, how it connects to the earlier requirements, and whether the posting says anything about visa sponsorship or work authorization. To round it out, use Google one more time to build me a short dated timeline of Rosie O’Donnell’s feud with Donald Trump with at least three dated moments from public sources, just as a quick check of the kind of entertainment-news research context that might overlap with this world. Please give me everything as a concise career brief with clear sections for training paths, Local 700 union entry, relevant current job examples, and the short timeline, and mention which pages you left open for me to review.
grader_reasoning:
R1: success - The agent successfully researched the media/entertainment IT engineer role. In step 54, the agent summarized three recurring requirements: 1) Core IT systems skills (networking, storage, Linux/Windows admin), 2) Media/post-production workflow knowledge (Avid/Adobe/Pro Tools ecosystems, NAS/SAN), and 3) Cloud, automation, and scripting (AWS/Azure, Python/Bash). The agent also identified two concrete training/certification routes: 1) Avid technical support certifications (ACSR for Media Composer/Pro Tools) and 2) Cloud certifications (AWS Certified Solutions Architect - Associate, CompTIA Cloud+). This information was sourced from the Google search results and the specific certification pages opened during the trajectory.

R2: success - The agent successfully navigated to the Motion Picture Editors Guild (IATSE Local 700) website, specifically the "Join West Coast" page. It identified the requirement for placement on the Industry Experience Roster (CSATF) as a prerequisite for union membership, detailed the specific work experience requirements (100 days of non-union work in the last two years for the Engineer classification), and provided information on initiation fees and the orientation process. The agent left the "Join West Coast" page open as requested. The final brief includes a clear section summarizing these union entry requirements.

R3: success - The agent successfully identified and opened two relevant live job postings in separate tabs: a "Media Systems Engineer II (Project Hire)" role at Disney (tab 5) and a "Network & Systems Engineer, Media Systems Engineering" role at Amazon (tab 6). The agent performed a check for visa sponsorship or work authorization language in both postings (steps 30 and 32) and correctly reported that no such language was found in the text of those specific postings. The final brief (step 54) includes the required details for these postings, including title, company, location, relevance to requirements, and the note regarding the absence of visa/work authorization language.

R4: success - The agent successfully researched the Rosie O'Donnell and Donald Trump feud and provided a timeline with dated milestones in its final brief (which was generated in the final step of the trajectory). The timeline includes:
1. 2006: The feud begins when O'Donnell critiques Trump on *The View*.
2. August 6, 2015: Trump references the feud during a Republican presidential debate.
3. December 20, 2016: O'Donnell tweets "FIRE COMEY," which Trump later references in 2017.
4. January 2017: O'Donnell tweets that Trump is "mentally unstable."
5. July 2026: Trump calls O'Donnell a "threat to humanity" on Truth Social, and O'Donnell responds on Instagram.

The requirement for at least three dated milestones is met.

R5: success - The agent provided a comprehensive career brief that includes all four requested sections: training paths, Local 700 union entry, relevant current job examples, and the Rosie O'Donnell/Donald Trump feud timeline. The brief is clearly labeled with sections. The agent explicitly mentions the pages left open for review (the Local 700 "Join West Coast" page, the initiation fee page, the CSATF page, and the two job postings for Disney and Amazon). The content is concise and directly addresses the user's request.
